pages.text: co-researchers [60], possesses superior thermo-mechanical and thermo-electrical properties and is the most commonly used SMAs. Nitinol SMAs have two unique properties: shape memory effect and superelasticity. The shape memory effect re- fers to the phenomenon that SMAs return back to their predetermined shapes upon heating. The superelasticity refers to the phenomenon that SMAs can undergo a large amount of inelastic deformation and recover their shape after unloading. By utilizing the shape memory effect of SMA wires, Song and Mo [61] proposed the concept of Intelligent Reinforced Concrete (IRC). The IRC used stranded mar- tensite SMA wires for post-tensioning (as shown in Fig. 11). By monitoring the elec- tric resistance change of the shape memory alloy wires, the strain distribution inside the concrete could be obtained. In the presence of cracks due to explosions or earthquakes, by electrically heating the SMA wires, the wire strands contracted and reduced the cracks. This self-rehabilitation could handle macro-sized cracks. The concrete structure was intelligent in the sense that it was able to detect self- rehabilitate. Sakai et al. [54] investigated self-restoration of a concrete beam using super elastic SMA wires. The experimental results revealed that the mortar beam with SMA wires recovered almost completely after incurring an extremely large crack. Similarly, shape memory polymer (SMP) materials were also proposed to be incorporated into cementitious matrix [57,58]. The basic concept is that: (1) crack- ing occurs in the cementitious material due to early age shrinkage, thermal effects and/or mechanical loading; (2) the shrinkage mechanism in the embedded shape memory polymer tendon (or tendons) is activated by heating, which results in crack closure and compressive stresses being developed across the closed crack faces. The closure of cracks can enhance the natural self-healing process and in general such closure serves to enhance the durability of the structural component. Based on the experiments, Jefferson et al. [58] concluded that the concept of post-tensioning mortar beams using oriented shrinkable polymer tendons was viable for crack clo- sure and low-level pre-stressing. It was also shown in their work that the most effective shape memory material for the tendons, as judged from a series of screen- ing tests, was polyethylene terephthalate (PET) Shrinktite. This material has a shrinkage potential of approximately 34 MPa under restrained conditions when heated to a temperature of 90 /C176C and allowed to cool. And the effect of heating com- bined with additional curing helped to increase the strength of the mortar by approximately 25%. 3. Comparison study and discussion Of all the proposed self-healing strategies, some of them focus and dig into the intrinsic self-healing potential of cementitious materials themselves, such as the unhydrated cementitious components inside and the capability of the matrix to provide cal- cium ions; while there are also some studies which try to endow cementitious matrices with self-healing capacity through the intro- duction of external chemicals or functional materials, like the embedded healing agent (different kinds of resin) and the shape memory materials. Combined use of more than one single method is also found [62]. About the healing agent and all the methods reviewed in this study, a summary can be discussed as follows. 3.1. Secondly, we should also bear in mind that certain hydration product is not stable in and after ele- vated temperature treatment, for example, Delayed Ettringite For- mation (DEF) as mentioned by many authors [75–81] , which also would lead to uncertainties of adopting this method. A more expli- cit comparison about the reviewed different self-healing strategies is summarized in Table 1 . 4. Self-healing in Engineered Cementitious Composite (ECC) 4.1. Engineered Cementitious Composite (ECC) It is well known that normal concrete is a brittle material, and the brittleness becomes even more pronounced with the ever- increasing concrete strength grade. This phenomenon has drawn increasing attention and the exploration of using ﬁbers to address this concern started very early [82,83] and has been lasting since it starts. There is a class of materials separate from FRC in that differ- ent magnitude of tensile ductility is achieved, often accompanied by a strain hardening response distinct from the tension response of FRC. Naam and Reinhardt [84] classiﬁed such materials as High Performance Fiber Reinforced Cementitious Composites (HPFRCC). Fig. 12 shows the comparison of uniaxial tensile stress-deforma- tion relation of concrete, FRC and HPFRCC. Recently two new clas- ses of HPFRCC emerge: (1) drastically improved toughness with improved ductility, represented by Ductal /C210[85], which has a high tensile strength of 12 MPa and a ductility of 0.02–0.06%; (2) signif- icantly increased ductility, represented by Engineered Cementi- tious Composite (or ECC for short) [86–88] . The development approach for these two different classes of materials is quite differ- ent. For Ductal, the approach is to employ a tightly packed dense matrix to increase both tensile and compressive strength of the material. Fiber is added to counteract the resulting high brittleness of the densiﬁed matrix. The dense matrix allows a strong bond with the ﬁber that results in a high post-cracking strength as long Table 1 A summarized comparison between different strategies. Review A review: Self-healing in cementitious materials and engineered cementitious composite as a self-healing material Min Wu a,⇑, Björn Johannesson a, Mette Geiker a,b a Department of Civil Engineering, Building 118, Technical University of Denmark, 2800 Lyngby, Denmark b Department of Structural Engineering, Norwegian University of Science and Technology, Trondheim, Norway article info Article history: Received 17 June 2011 Received in revised form 29 August 2011 Accepted 30 August 2011 Available online 14 December 2011 Keywords: Self-healing Cementitious materials Healing agent Healing strategy ECC Micro-crack Crack-width control abstract Cementitious materials are the most widely used building materials all over the word. However, deteri- oration is inevitable even since the very beginning of the service life, then maintenance and repair work, which are often labor- and capital-intensive, would be followed. Thus, self-healing of the affected cemen- titious materials is of great importance. Self-healing phenomenon in cementitious materials has been noticed and been studying for a long time. The possible mechanisms for self-healing phenomenon in cementitious materials, which are summarized based on substantial experimental studies and practical experience, are reviewed. In order to endow or enhance the self-healing property of cementitious mate- rials, many different and innovative strategies have been proposed and developed during the past dec- ades. Then the different strategies employed for the self-healing of cementitious materials, i.e. the use of hollow ﬁbers, microencapsulation, expansive agents and mineral admixtures, bacteria and shape memory materials, are reviewed and summarized. A comparison study is conducted subsequently on dif- ferent strategies to self-healing and on different healing agents used as well. Engineered Cementitious Composite (ECC) is a new type of cement-based materials, which has unique properties compared with traditional cementitious materials. Further, a summary about the research status of self-healing on ECC is given. It shows that ECC bears great potential in realizing effective self-healing due to its unique micro- crack behavior and tight crack-width control property combined with its relatively high percentage of cementitious components and low water–binder ratio. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. Acknowledgements . . . . . . . . . . ......................................................................................... 5 8 1 References . ......................................................................................................... 5 8 1 1. Introduction Many concrete structures including infrastructures suffer from serious deterioration all over the world. Thus inspection and main- tenance techniques for concrete structures have therefore become the focus of increasing attention. However, the implementation of continuous inspection and maintenance is difﬁcult, especially in the case of large-scale concrete structures such as infrastructures, owing to the considerable amount of labor and funds required. On the other hand, repair may be difﬁcult or impossible to be exe- cuted because of existing conditions such as the location of the damage in the affected structure. Many infrastructures such as highways and tunnels are also in continuous service and in such cases repair work becomes very difﬁcult. Moreover, even if such re- pair work were possible in principle, the cost and amount of labor required for diagnosis and repair work can be prohibitive in the case of large-scale infrastructures. Under such circumstances, automatic repair, or rather self-healing, of harmful cracks without onerous labor and capital requirements of affected structures could be of great attraction. Development of artiﬁcial self-healing materials is inspired by different materials in our nature. A typical example of self-healing phenomenon is damaged skins of trees and animals can autono- mously heal themselves. van Breugel [1] summarized the research history of self-healing phenomena in cementitious materials: self- healing or autogenous healing of cracks in fractured concrete has been noticed by the French Academy of Science in 1836 already in water retaining structures, culverts and pipes [2]. The self-heal- ing phenomenon was studied already by Hyde and Smith [3,4] at the end of the nineteenth century. A more systematic analysis of healing phenomena dates back to 1926 and was executed by Glanville [5]. Already at that time a distinction was made between self-healing and self-sealing. In the ﬁrst case the original strength of the concrete is completely recovered, whereas in the second case leaking cracks will be closed but no strength recovery is obtained. Glanville’s ﬁrst studies have been followed by studies of cracks in bridges [6,7]. Jacobsen and Sellevold [8] observed self-healing in concrete specimens that had been exposed to freeze–thaw cycles. Self-healing of leaking cracks were studied extensively by Clear [9], Hearn [2,3,10] and Edvardsen [11]. Based on substantial experiment studies and practical experi- ence, it is believed that self-healing of cracks in cementitious mate- rials is a combination of complicated chemical and physical processes. As revealed in most of the literatures studied, several possible causes can be responsible for the self-healing phenomena (schematically shown in Fig. 1 ) [12]: a. Formation of calcium carbonate or calcium hydroxide. b. Blocking cracks by impurities in the water and loose con- crete particles resulting from crack spalling. c. Further hydration of the unreacted cement or cementitious materials. Fig. 1. Possible mechanisms for self-healing in cementitious materials [12]. Fig. 2. Cracks are ﬁlled with white residue. (a) White residue ﬁlling the cracks after curing clearly observed on the surface

d. Expansion of the hydrated cementitious matrix in the crack ﬂanks (swelling of C–S–H). Of all these proposed causes, the primary mechanism is be- lieved to be attributed to the crystallization of calcium carbonate [11,13–16] . This view is supported by the fact that precipitated cal- cium carbonate can often be observed at the outside surfaces of the crack as some white residue, which is shown in Fig. 2a macroscop- ically and Fig. 2 b microscopically. As one of the cement hydration products dissolved in water, the calcium hydroxide is liberated and dissipated along the cracking surfaces. Then free calcium ions from cement hydration react with dissolved carbon dioxide, so ‘‘self- healed’’ crystals are formed, growing at both surfaces of the cracks and ﬁnally ﬁlling into the gaps. The chemical reaction process could be described as follows [11]: H2O þ CO2 () H2CO3 ()Hþ þ HCO/C0 3 () 2Hþ þ CO2/C0 3 ð1Þ Ca2þ þ CO2/C0 3 () CaCO3 ðpHwater > 8Þð 2Þ Ca2þ þ HCO/C0 3 () CaCO3 þ Hþ ð7:5 < pHwater < 8Þð 3Þ According to the early work by Neville [17], self-healing is claimed to be caused mainly by further hydration of unhydrated cementi- tious components. However, later it was discovered that this only applies to very young concrete and it was concluded that the forma- tion of calcium carbonate is the most likely cause of self-healing at later ages [18]. Engineered Cementitious Composite (ECC) is a new type of ce- ment-based engineering material. The special properties of ECC distinguish itself from the traditional cementitious materials, such as the high ductility, the micro-cracking behavior and tight crack- with control property. Moreover, the unique characteristics also endow ECC with a great potential to realize effective self-healing. Although the history of ECC is only about two decade, it could be very promising in engineering application. Thus, a separate section, Section 4, is devoted to the basics of ECC and the development of self-healing in ECC materials. 2. Self-healing strategies in cementitious materials Damaged skins and tissues can be self-healed, because the host can assimilate nutrients to produce new substitutes healing the damaged parts. Accordingly, for the self-healing of cementitious materials, the essence is to provide necessary prod- ucts which can then ﬁll in the cracks when damage happens. With respect to how to endow composite materials with self-healing property and improve the self-healing efﬁciency, many experimental studies and explorations have been conducted and generated a lot of innovative strategies during the past few decades. Some of their works are summarized as below. 2.1. Hollow ﬁbers The idea of using hollow ﬁbers (sometimes referred to as hollow pipettes or tubes depending on the diameter) is to store some functional components inside of the hollow ﬁbers which will be embedded into a composite matrix. Thus when damage or cracking happens, under certain stimulus, the functional components, or rather healing agents, will ﬂow out and heal the cracks subsequently. Self-healing using hollow ﬁbers embedded within an engineering structure, similar to the arteries in a natural system, has been investigated at different length scales in different engineering materials by various authors, for example, in bulk polymers [19], in polymeric composites [20–23] . The suitability of hollow ﬁbers or tubes in allowing the release of healing was in most cases proven. In order to quickly and easily observe the internal dam

Crack Mouth Opening Displacement (CMOD) was 0.03–2 mm, followed by removal of the load. Then the cracked specimens were cured for the second duration for the development of self-healing capacity. The results showed compared to the refer- ence sample in which no healing agent was used, specimens with diluted and non-diluted alkali-silica solution as the healing agent obtained average strength recovery ratio of 1.1 and 1.5 or so, respectively. While for the specimens with epoxy resin, the strength recovery ratio showed little improvement. This recovery value was much lower than that obtained by directly mixing the resin and manually injecting it into the cracks, which was in the order of three times better recovery. The author attributed this discrepancy to the insufﬁcient mixing and stirring of the two components, thus less resin hardened leading to the low recovery ratio. It is also reasonable to think that some of the epoxy may still remain in the pipes as one end is sealed. The testing regime of Joseph’s work [29] was similar despite of some minor differences. Curved plastic tubes with outer diameter 4 mm and in- ner diameter 3 mm were used as the healing agent carrier. Ethyl cyanoacrylate was used as the healing agent. It was concluded that an ethyl cyanoacrylate based heal- ing agent supplied via an external supply system was capable of achieving a suc- cessful self-healing. The post-cracked stiffness, peak load and ductility were all observed to increase post-healing. Observations made during and after testing clearly indicated that, ethyl cyanoacrylate glue was capable of penetrating a signif- icant area of the crack surfaces under the inﬂuence of capillary suction forces and gravity. Different from the external healing agent supply system, internal encapsulation of healing adhesives was also studied [30–32] . Li et al. [30] explored the feasibility of using this method to embody ECC with self-healing property, which will be dis- cussed in more details in Section 4.2. Thao et al. [31] studied the selection of healing agent, carrier and the conﬁnement of a healing agent and the implementation of this encapsulation method in concrete. In their study, a one part low viscosity epoxy resin with the viscosity range of 250–500 cPa.s was chosen as the healing agent. For the car- rying tubes, comparison study was conducted between hollow Perspex and glass tubes. It showed that glass tubes were more suitable due to the chemical inertness to epoxy resin and the brittleness which could be easier to rupture when cracks were initiated, ensuring the leakage of the healing agent contained inside. Dimension study of the glass tubes showed that the optimal inner and outer diameter were 4 mm and 6 mm respectively. Then experiment was conducted to prove that the idea could work. Specimen and testing setup was given as Fig. 5 . The control specimens were loaded to failure to determine the load–displacement curve. Specimen with glass tubes were tested beyond the point where an audible ‘pop’ sound was heard, which indicated fracturing of the glass tube. The load was held until leakage of the epoxy was observed on the bottom surface of the specimen. The specimen was then un- loaded and left unloaded for 4 days before being loaded again using the same dis- placement rate. The test was terminated soon after more leakage of epoxy healing agent was observed. The load was then removed and the specimen left to heal for 3 days.before being loaded again and test

Then the healing agent contacts the embedded catalyst, triggering polymerization and ensuring the closure of the near-by cracks. A typical ruptured microcapsule im- age is shown in Fig. 7 b. Boh and Šumiga [38] reviewed the application of microencapsulated additives in building and construction materials. In order to facilitate the further hydration of the matrix, different additives are encapsulated into microcapsules which are then embedded into the matrix. Tomiuchi and Nishihama [39] patented applica- tions of microencapsulated water in the production of cement-based building boards with uniform strength. Typical compositions consisted of cement, mixed with ﬁber (e.g. asbestos, synthetic ﬁber) and microcapsules containing water for hardening. The building boards with uniform strength were manufactured on a belt conveyer by compression-molding, resulting in cement hardening. In another invention [40], high strength cement-based building boards were manufactured from a mixture of hydraulic material (gypsum), ﬁbrous material, additives, and gel- atin microcapsules containing water glass as a setting accelerator. Microencapsulat- ed or gelled water was used in cartridges of quick-setting cement [41]. A premixed mortar mixture was patented, consisting of cement and microcapsules containing water [42]. In a patent by Okamoto et al. [43] on manufacturing concrete and mor- tar mixes, water was encapsulated in an acrylate superabsorbent polymer. In the production of concrete or mortar, the water was released from the superabsorbent to promote hydration of the surrounding cement. They also discussed about some other application ﬁelds of this microencapsulation in building materials, i.e. ﬁre- prooﬁng, freeze and freeze–thaw resistance, expansion and degradation resistance, reduction of hydration heat release, etc. In the work conducted by Nishiwaki [44], microcapsules ﬁlled with epoxy resin as one component of the healing agent (the shell material of urea–formaldehyde formalin with the diameter of 20–70 lm) and microcapsules with acrylic resin as a hardener (the shell material of gelatin with the diameter of 125–297 lm) were used. Both compression and splitting tests were carried out to conﬁrm the problems of this method for self-healing. The results showed: (1) it was difﬁcult to harden the two-component healing agent; (2) the quantity of healing agent ﬁlled in the micro- capsules was limited to a very small amount; (3) bond strength between the micro- capsules and the matrix needed to be stronger than the strength of the microcapsules. In order to solve these problems: (1) one component healing agent as the core material might be used; (2) the diameter of the microcapsule should be big enough; (3) the bond strength between the shell materials and cementitious matrix should be improved. Finally it was concluded that there were still a lot of technical problems. It is also reported that microcapsules incorporating sodium silicate were used [45]. In the reported research, concrete was ﬁrstly loaded to the point of almost breaking. Then the load was removed and followed by curing for about 1 week. Measurements showed the specimen with 2% microencapsulated sodium silicate proceeded to recover 26% of its original strength, while the reference concrete only recovered 10%. It was believed that the strength recovery ratio could be even higher by increasing the quantity of the healing agent. 2.3. Expansive agents and mineral admixtures The formation o

as a ﬁber with high strength is utilized. While for ECC, it is to create synergistic interactions between the ﬁber, matrix, and interface to maximize the tensile ductility through the development of closely spaced multiple micro-cracks which help to release the stresses generated. It is reported that ECC has a typical moderate tensile strength of 4–6 MPa and a high ductility of more than 3%, which is hundreds times that of normal concrete [89,90]. ECC is a class of specially designed, ultra ductile ﬁber reinforced cementitious composites originally invented in the early 1990s [91] and continuously evolved over the last two decade [90].I ti s characterized by high ductility in the strain range of 3–7%, tight crack width of around 60 lm and relatively low ﬁber content of 2% or less by volume [90,92]. The most fundamental mechanical property of ECC is its metal-like property, i.e. the ability to carry higher loads after ﬁrst cracking while undergoing further deforma- tions. Based on micromechanics design, the ﬁbers used in ECC are tailored to work with the matrix for the purpose of constraining localized brittle fracture and guaranteeing more uniform distribu- tion of micro-cracks. Due to the slip-hardening behavior of ﬁbers, ECC can take increasing load that generates new cracks at other locations. A typical tensile stress–strain curve of ECC is shown in Fig. 13 . It can be found that ﬁrst cracking in ECC is followed by increasing stress accompanied by a rise in strain; while for tradi- tional FRC, tensile load capacity drops as a single crack enlarges during tension softening ( Fig. 12 ). The crack width is another important parameter that affects the durability of a concrete structure. Fig. 13 also shows the tight crack width control of ECC. As can be observed that after the tensile deformation increases to around 1% strain, the early micro-cracks stop widening and remain more or less constant with the crack width around 60 lm, which is much smaller than the typical crack width observed in steel reinforced concrete and normal ﬁber rein- forced concrete. Moreover, the self-control of crack width can be seen as an intrinsic property of ECC materials, rather than depend- ing on steel reinforcement ratio and structural dimensions [93,94]. 4.2. Self-healing in ECC Li et al. [30] reviewed the self-healing concept of bleeding mechanism proposed by Dry [25] as mentioned in Section 2.1, which is to release chemicals that seal the tensile cracks, followed by air curing of the released chemicals in the cracks, leading to regaining of mechanical properties of the uncracked composite. He argued that the self-healing mechanism described could not work in normal concrete, cement or even ﬁber reinforced concrete, because the width of the tensile cracks in such materials could not be easily tuned. Localized fracture led to continued increases in crack width under decreasing tensile load, and rapidly exhausted the amount of chemical available for crack sealing and composite re-healing. Thus, for the proposed self-healing concept to work, it was critical that the tensile crack width be controlled, and it must be limited to within tens of micrometers. Otherwise, very large hollow glass tubes would be needed, which in turn negatively modiﬁed the mechanical properties of the composite. This is in good agreement with many other researches conducted in this ﬁeld, which also state the importance of the crack width [9,11,95–99] . Since the tight controlled crack
